concepts.lesson: Gravitation is the force of attraction between any two objects in the universe. This force pulls objects toward each other and keeps planets in orbit around the Sun.
concepts.real_example: An apple falling from a tree is an example of gravitational force.
key_points: Acts between all objects in the universe
Causes objects to fall towards Earth

concepts.lesson: The universal law of gravitation states that every object in the universe attracts every other object with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
concepts.real_example: The Earth attracts the Moon due to gravitational force.
key_points: Force depends on mass of objects
Applicable to all objects in the universe

concepts.lesson: Free fall refers to the motion of an object when it falls towards the Earth under the influence of gravity alone.
concepts.real_example: A stone dropped from a height falls due to free fall.
key_points: Acceleration due to gravity acts on them

concepts.lesson: Acceleration due to gravity is the acceleration gained by an object when it falls toward the Earth due to gravitational force.
concepts.real_example: Objects near Earth's surface fall with acceleration of about 9.8 m/s².
key_points: Acts towards the center of Earth
Independent of mass of the object

concepts.lesson: Mass is the amount of matter present in an object. It is a measure of inertia and remains constant everywhere.
concepts.real_example: A person's mass remains the same on Earth and on the Moon.
key_points: Amount of matter in an object

concepts.lesson: Weight is the force with which the Earth attracts an object. It depends on the mass of the object and acceleration due to gravity.
concepts.real_example: A 10 kg object has a weight of about 98 N on Earth.

concepts.lesson: Pressure is defined as the force acting per unit area. Smaller areas produce greater pressure for the same force.
concepts.real_example: Sharp knives cut easily because they exert more pressure.

concepts.lesson: Buoyancy is the upward force exerted by a fluid on an object placed in it.
concepts.real_example: A plastic bottle floats on water due to buoyant force.

concepts.lesson: An object floats in a fluid if its density is less than the density of the fluid, and sinks if its density is greater.
concepts.real_example: A cork floats in water but an iron nail sinks.

concepts.lesson: Archimedes' principle states that when an object is immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by it.
concepts.real_example: Ships float because the buoyant force balances their weight.
key_points: Upward force equals weight of displaced fluid
